- **Age + KO loss history flag:** 33 years old with 2 KO/TKO losses in career (Quarantillo R2, and implicitly an early loss to Moicano via submission). The Quarantillo KO (R2, 4:30) was late-ish in Round 2 — not a clean R1 chin alarm, but notable. At 33 in lightweight, approaching the **age cliff per Rule 9.7** (MW/LHW cliff is 36, but LW men cliff is 35 — Hernandez is 2 years away, not yet firing).
- **Momentum signal:** 4-fight win streak, including 2 recent KO finishes. However, opponent quality caveat applies — Hooper is a grappler adapting to striking, Ferreira is past his prime. **Trending up against below-ranked opposition — discount heavily per Rule 3.2.**
- **Intangibles:** Layoff 224 days (same as Garcia — −6% penalty, symmetric). No weight miss data, no short-notice flag.
<!--END-->

<!--HEAD2HEAD-->
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

**The matrix read:** Garcia is a Pressure Wrestler (FORWARD); Hernandez is a Power Striker (FORWARD). Per the style matrix (Rule 5.1), **Pressure Wrestler beats Power Striker** — the structural logic is that Hernandez needs space and distance to generate power, and Garcia's ability to close distance and get to the body/clinch neutralizes that. The critical variable is **TD defense** (Rule 5.2): Hernandez sits at 73% TD defense — in the 60–75% "coin flip" zone. That keeps the matchup from being a clean wrestle-dominant outcome, but still leans Garcia structurally. Garcia's 3.13 TD avg at 44% accuracy against a 73% TD defense suggests he'll get *some* takedowns but won't dominate position completely.

**The striking exchange problem:** Both fighters are FORWARD, both absorb nearly identically (Garcia 4.54, Hernandez 4.50 SApM), and both have mediocre strike defense (61% vs 59%). When two forward-pressure fighters meet, expect a brawl and early finish potential. Garcia's last-3 shows volume output that has cratered — he averaged 1.06 SLpM in his last three fights, which means he's either been outgamed in striking range or has tactically withdrawn to wrestling-first. Against Hernandez, that approach could work: close distance, body lock, grind. However, Garcia's own chin vulnerability is on record — the Dawson finish in R2 when his output went to near-zero suggests he gets hurt when he can't dictate pace. If Hernandez lands a clean shot on the entry, Garcia's durability under a power striker is genuinely uncertain.

**Net intangible delta:** Both fighters share identical layoffs (224 days, −6% each) — this cancels out. Garcia's one recent KO loss (Dawson) is partially offset by the fact he responded with two wins, including a KO finish of his own over Gordon. Hernandez's 4-fight win streak looks impressive until you see the opponent quality: Holobaugh, Hubbard, Hooper, Ferreira — none ranked. Garcia's wins include Gordon (a serviceable veteran) and Pichel (solid veteran). Net intangible delta: **slight lean toward Garcia** based on opponent quality in recent wins, but neither fighter has a decisive edge here. Path to victory for Garcia: close distance immediately, use 76% TDD to negate any Hernandez scramble attempts, tie up in the clinch, drag to the floor, control rounds on top. His one KO win (Gordon, R3) shows he can finish if the opening is there. Path to victory for Hernandez: keep the fight standing in the first 30 seconds of each round before Garcia closes distance, land the right hand or hook on Garcia's entry, and exploit Garcia's historically low striking output (which leaves him on the back foot defensively when his wrestling is neutralized). His two recent KO finishes show he can end fights standing.

**Flip conditions (Rule 15.1):** If Garcia's TDD in recent fights is shown to be declining (it's held at 76% career with no clear drop, so this is stable), switch toward Hernandez. If Hernandez's KO power in the last-3 is driven by one-shot moments against outgunned opponents (likely), discount his finish threat vs. Garcia. If Garcia's layoff is injury-related, push to NO BET due to deeper rust concern.
<!--END-->

<!--ENDINGS-->
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**Section 8 math anchors:**

Garcia KO adjustment on Hernandez:
- Garcia's base KO rate: 1 KO in last 10 = 10% career KO rate (low sample, use conservatively)
- Hernandez Str.Def: 59% → (1 − 0.59) = 0.41
- No CHIN CRACKED flag on Hernandez (only 1 recent KO loss, not two)
- Adjusted KO prob (Garcia over Hernandez): 0.10 × 0.41 × 1.0 = ~4% (very low — Garcia is not a KO threat)

Hernandez KO adjustment on Garcia:
- Hernandez base KO rate: 3 KOs in last 10 = 30%
- Garcia Str.Def: 61% → (1 − 0.61) = 0.39
- No formal CHIN CRACKED on Garcia (1 KO loss in last 3 years but only 1 flag met), but apply 1.2 modifier per Rule 8.6 (Str.Def trending — actually flat, so standard 1.0; however Dawson KO noted — use 1.15 conservative bump)
- Adjusted KO prob (Hernandez over Garcia): 0.30 × 0.39 × 1.15 = ~13.5%

Submission adjustment (Garcia over Hernandez):
- Garcia sub rate: 1 sub in last 10 = 10%
- Hernandez TDD: 73% → (1 − 0.73) = 0.27
- Sub def proxy: Hernandez has 1 sub loss (Moicano) out of 8 total losses → proxy = 1 − (1/8) = 0.875 (strong sub defense)
- Adjusted sub prob: 0.10 × 0.27 × (1 − 0.875) = 0.10 × 0.27 × 0.125 = ~0.3% (negligible)

Decision probability (Rule 8.4):
= 1 − 0.04 (Garcia KO) − 0.135 (Hernandez KO) − 0.003 (Garcia sub) ≈ **82%** raw decision probability before style-matchup adjustment.

Style adjustment: The FORWARD vs. FORWARD brawl tendency and both fighters' history of decisions (Garcia has 4 decisions in last 10; Hernandez has 3 decisions in last 10, but also 3 KOs) — adjust decision probability down to ~65%, distributing the remainder across finishes.

---

**#1 — Rafa Garcia wins by Unanimous Decision, Round 3**
Probability: 40%
Why: Garcia's structural wrestling advantage and elite 76% TDD make him the map-favorable fighter when this goes deep. His pattern of grinding out decisions (4 in last 10 fights, multiple R3 full-distance wins against Guida, Maheshate, Pichel) fits a fight where he closes distance, controls clinch and cage time, and edges volume on the cards. Hernandez's 73% TDD means he's not completely shut down on the fence, but Garcia's pressure tends to accumulate over rounds.

**#2 — Alexander Hernandez wins by KO/TKO, Rounds 1–2**
Probability: 22%
Why: Hernandez's recent power-shot finishes (Hooper R1, Ferreira R2) show he can end fights early if he catches Garcia coming in. Garcia's entry on takedown attempts leaves him momentarily exposed, and his SLpM of 0.6 in the Dawson loss showed he can be shut down offensively before being finished. If Hernandez lands clean on Garcia's entry in the first two rounds, the fight ends.

**#3 — Alexander Hernandez wins by Unanimous Decision, Round 3**
Probability: 20%
Why: If Hernandez's TD defense (73%) proves sufficient to mostly neutralize Garcia's wrestling — preventing takedowns in the 60–75% range means Garcia doesn't dominate — then Hernandez's edge in striking output when the fight is standing can accumulate over three rounds. His three recent decision wins suggest he can go the distance when opponents are competitive. This scenario requires Garcia's volume suppression (last-3 SLpM ~1.06) to continue.

**#4 — Rafa Garcia wins by KO/TKO, Round 3**
Probability: 10%
Why: Garcia stopped Jared Gordon by KO in Round 3 in his most recent fight (Sep 2025), showing late-fight finishing ability despite being a decision-heavy fighter. If he controls the majority of the fight via wrestling and wears Hernandez down, a ground-and-pound finish is plausible but not the likely path — Hernandez's durability (no recent KO-only loss pattern) limits this.

**#5 — Rafa Garcia wins by Submission, Round 2–3**
Probability: 5%
Why: Garcia's sub avg is 0.5/15min and he has 1 sub win in last 10, but Hernandez's TDD (73%) and minimal sub loss history make a submission unlikely unless Garcia completely dominates positionally. Low-probability outcome included because Garcia's wrestling archetype keeps submission as a theoretical secondary option.

*Remaining ~3% covers draws, doctor stoppages, or unlisted scenarios.*
<!--END-->

<!--BETTING-->
**TIER: LEAN**

**Primary Pick: Rafa Garcia — Confidence: 57%**

*(Note: Recommendation is LEAN only, not PLAY, due to: MEDIUM data tier on both fighters capping confidence at 67%, below-ranked opponent quality for Hernandez's win streak making the market's reprice to −150 suspicious, and the acknowledged uncertainty around Garcia's recent volume collapse. Market-tier disagreement also fires a −5% confidence adjustment per Rule 10.11 — explained below.)*

---

**Edge Breakdown:**

Line: Garcia +105 (current) / Hernandez −150 (current)

Garcia implied prob: 100 / (105 + 100) = **48.8%**
Hernandez implied prob: 150 / (150 + 100) = **60.0%**
Combined implied: 48.8 + 60.0 = **108.8%** (8.8% vig — high)

De-vig (Rule 11.2):
- True prob Garcia: 48.8 / 108.8 = **44.9%**
- True prob Hernandez: 60.0 / 108.8 = **55.1%**

My assessed probability for Garcia: **57%** (style matrix structural edge: Pressure Wrestler over Power Striker; elite TDD 76% vs. Hernandez 73% TD defense in coin-flip zone; Garcia's opponent quality in recent wins edges Hernandez's; symmetric layoff cancels out)

**Edge on Garcia: +57% − 44.9% = +12.1%**

⚠️ Per Rule 11.3: Edge above 12% triggers REVIEW REQUIRED — re-checking the read:
- The line has moved heavily toward Hernandez (opened +105 for Hernandez, now −150 — a 255-cent swing toward Hernandez). This is a major movement.
- This is NOT confirmed RLM (the market data explicitly states RLM = NO). The line moved *with* public/market sentiment toward Hernandez.
- The market's repricing of Hernandez is substantial. Under Rule 10.2, the opening line (Garcia −130 / Hernandez +105 implied Garcia ~56.5% true prob) was the sharper estimate. The drift toward Hernandez suggests public money on a hot 4-fight streak.
- Per Rule 10.10, Hernandez is a case of a fighter on a marketable winning streak against below-ranked opposition — exactly the public-favorite fade scenario.
- After re-examination: the structural read still favors Garcia. The market has moved dramatically against the opening line without a clear sharp catalyst (RLM is NO, so this is public-driven). The statistical edge remains.

Adjusted assessed probability after market disagreement penalty: **57% − 5% = 52%** (Rule 10.11: stat-read disagrees with market direction; market moved toward Hernandez, our read is toward Garcia). However, market movement is public-not-sharp per RLM = NO, so applying only the standard −5% penalty, not a cap.

**Revised edge: 52% − 44.9% = +7.1%** (still above 5% threshold — PLAY territory, but data tier cap and "would I bet $1000" check apply)

**Data tier cap:** Both fighters MEDIUM → confidence capped at 67%. Confidence after all adjustments = 52% → within LEAN tier (55–60% would be lean; 52% is technically marginal-to-NO BET).

**Stake Recommendation:**
- Decimal odds for Garcia at +105: (105/100) + 1 = 2.05
- Quarter Kelly: [(0.07 × 2.05) − (1 − 0.52)] / (2.05 − 1) × 0.25
  = [(0.1435) − (0.48)] / (1.05) × 0.25
  = [−0.3365 / 1.05] × 0.25
  = −0.321 × 0.25 = **−0.08 (negative Kelly — mathematically −EV at revised assessed prob)**

⚠️ Quarter Kelly is **negative** at 52% assessed probability. Per Rule 11.4: negative Kelly = do not place the bet regardless of confidence feel.

**Revised recommendation: LEAN only — NO PLAY at current line.**

The bet only becomes mathematically +EV at Garcia assessed probability ≥ ~55% (breakeven given the de-vigged true prob of 44.9%). With the market-disagreement penalty applied, we're at 52% — inside the noise.

**Target line:** Garcia needs to be available at **+120 or longer** for a clean +5% edge play at assessed 57% base probability (pre-market-penalty). At +115: implied 46.5%, de-vigged ~43%, edge at 57% base = +14% (strong on paper but requires dismissing the market penalty entirely). At current +105, the penalty-adjusted read doesn't clear the threshold.

---

**Data Tiers:**
- Rafa Garcia: **MEDIUM**
- Alexander Hernandez: **MEDIUM**
- Active confidence caps: MEDIUM tier → max 67%. Kelly turning negative at penalty-adjusted 52% → no stake warranted.

---

**Market Read:**
- Open: Garcia −130 / Hernandez +105 → True prob Garcia ~56.5%, Hernandez ~43.5% at open
- Current: Garcia +105 / Hernandez −150 → True prob Garcia ~44.9%, Hernandez ~55.1%
- Movement: **Massive swing toward Hernandez (−255 cents on Hernandez's line)**
- RLM status: **NO** — movement aligns with public money direction (Hernandez is the hotter name with 4-fight win streak)
- Public %: Not available
- Assessment: Market has repriced heavily toward Hernandez on public action — the opening line (which favored Garcia at ~56.5%) was closer to the structural read. Current line is public-inflated. Garcia at +105 represents a fade-the-public opportunity *in principle*, but the Kelly math at penalty-adjusted probability doesn't support a stake. **Monitor: if Garcia drifts to +120 or longer, the bet becomes mathematically valid.**

---

**Hedge Verdict:**

Combined implied: Garcia +105 → 48.8% + Hernandez −150 → 60.0% = **108.8%**

**NO ARB.** Combined implied is 108.8% (8.8% vig). No arbitrage opportunity. Take value on best available line (Garcia at +105 if betting) — no hedge warranted, and hedging here would be a guaranteed loss.

---

**Props Worth Considering:**

"Fight goes to Decision" prop: My calculated decision probability via Section 8.4 math = ~65%. If a sportsbook is offering decision YES at implied probability below 55%, there may be prop value — both fighters have heavy decision histories and the FORWARD vs. FORWARD dynamic in this specific low-volume matchup (both fighters averaging ~1-2 SLpM recently) favors grinding. **De-vig any prop line before placing — prop vig typically 8–12%, requiring 5%+ assessed edge to play.**

"Fight ends in Round 1" NO: Given both fighters' recent trend toward later finishes (Garcia's only recent finish was R3; Hernandez's last two were R1 and R2 but against a grappler and a faded vet), Round 1 finish is low probability (~12–15%). If NO is available at implied probability below 70%, potential value exists.

---

**Final Recommendation: LEAN Garcia (+105) — NO formal stake.**

The structural read favors Garcia, the market has moved dramatically on public action (not sharp money), and the opening line was closer to reality. However, the penalty-adjusted assessed probability (52%) pushes the Kelly criterion negative at +105, meaning there is no mathematically defensible stake size at the current price. If Garcia drifts to **+115 or longer**, re-evaluate — at +120 with 57% base assessed probability, the bet becomes a genuine PLAY.

**Would I bet $1000 of my own money on Garcia at +105? No — the Kelly is negative at penalty-adjusted probability. Pass on a stake; track the line.**
<!--END-->

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Davey Grant -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (6+ UFC fights, full career splits available, but last-3 sample has notable stat volatility; last fight was a R1 sub loss). **Archetype: Counter-Striker / Clinch-Pressure hybrid.** Career 5.14 SLpM with 44% accuracy and 63% TDD suggests a volume-capable fighter who works best in tight. **FORWARD tag.**

- **Stat-trend ALERT — Striking collapse in last-3:** SLpM last-3: (2.92 + 0.20 + 2.60) / 3 = **1.91/min vs career 5.14/min — TRENDING DOWN (−63%).** This is a dramatic output drop. Accuracy last-3: (34.6% + 60.0% + 54.2%) / 3 ≈ **49.6% vs career 44%** — FLAT/slightly up, but the volume collapse is the dominant signal. Grant is landing at a much lower rate in recent fights.

- **Grappling: near-dormant in last 3.** Career TD avg is 1.03/15min — already modest. Last-3 TD avg: 0.0 across all three fights. Either his wrestling is not translating at this stage of his career or opponents are negating his entries. Sub attempts at 0.2/15min career, 0 in last 3. He is functionally a striker at this point.

- **AGE CLIFF ACTIVE — 40 years old, bantamweight.** Per Rule 9.7: men Strawweight–Welterweight cliff = 35. Grant is 5 years past the cliff. Penalty: −5% per year capped at −15%. Cap fires: **−15% intangible penalty from age alone.** This is the single largest signal in this fight.

- **CHIN CRACKED FLAG — checking criteria:** (1) Most recent loss = R1 submission to Charles Jourdain (3:05 R1 — not a KO, but a R1 finish). (2) Career has sustained multiple losses including decision and finish losses. (3) Absorbs 3.82 SApM career, Str. Def. 55%. Last-3 SApM data not directly computable from provided splits alone. Not enough KO-specific data to formally flag CHIN CRACKED, but the R1 sub loss to Jourdain — who is not primarily a submission fighter — suggests either a gassing issue at this age or a defensive lapse under early pressure. **Flag: R1 DURABILITY CONCERN (not full CHIN CRACKED without more KO data).**

- **Layoff: 189 days** (~6.3 months). Per Rule 9.2: 180–270 days = mild ring rust risk, **−3% penalty.** At age 40, this compounds with the age penalty rather than being independent.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Adrian Luna Martinetti -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: LOW** — **1 UFC fight only.** Career stats (11.00 SLpM, 65% accuracy, 7.80 SApM, 48% strike defense, 100% TDD) are derived from a single 3-round UFC bout vs Mark Vologdin. Sample of 1 fight makes ALL career UFC averages statistically unreliable. Per Rule 2.2 and 2.4: confidence cap fires at **60% maximum.** All analysis weighted heavily on that single fight plus the pre-UFC record (17-1).

- **That single UFC fight (vs Vologdin) paints a specific picture but must be discounted:** Last-3 (sample of 1) shows SLpM 2.87, Str.Acc 57.3%, 0 TDs, 0 subs — which is far below the "career" 11.00 SLpM. The massive gap between single-fight "career" stats and the actual recorded fight suggests the career aggregate may be inflated by a small number of shorter fights or statistical anomalies. **Use the actual fight split (2.87 SLpM, 57.3% accuracy) as the operative number, not the career 11.00 SLpM.**

- **Archetype derived from limited data: Pressure Striker / Volume Striker (tentative).** The 17-1 pre-UFC record and the UFC career striking numbers (if taken at face value) suggest an aggressive output-focused fighter. However, the actual recorded UFC split shows a more measured 2.87 SLpM — consistent with a disciplined performance against a measured opponent. **Tag: FORWARD (tentative, LOW confidence). Pre-UFC archetype only.**

- **100% TDD in UFC career** — meaningful caveat: derived from a single fight in which 0 takedowns were attempted against him. This number is a placeholder, not a proven defensive skill. Cannot assess real TDD without more data. State as: **TDD: INSUFFICIENT DATA.**

- **Age advantage: 30 years old** — no age cliff penalty. Physical prime. 10-year age gap vs Grant is a significant structural advantage in terms of recovery, chin durability, and late-round performance. **Layoff: 200 days** (~6.7 months). Per Rule 9.2: **−3% ring rust penalty.** Identical layoff context to Grant.

- **Pre-UFC record: 17-1** — quality of opposition unknown. Without Tapology intangibles, cannot assess whether this is a regional can-crusher or legitimate international competition. **Tag: LOW confidence on pre-UFC signal.** The UFC debut adjustment (Rule 9.5) does not apply — he already has 1 UFC fight. However, he is functionally in his second-fight adjustment phase with a single UFC bout of data.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

**The structural picture: Age cliff vs. inexperience, both fighting off thin data.** The matrix analysis for this fight is fundamentally constrained by data quality. Grant brings 6+ UFC fights of career data but his last-3 trend represents a fighter in steep decline — 1.91 SLpM in recent output vs. a 5.14 career average represents a 63% volume drop. He is not fighting like the same man who KO'd Jonathan Martinez and Louis Smolka. Luna Martinetti has 1 UFC fight, making every style-matchup call conditional. That said, the age gap (40 vs. 30) combined with Grant's striking output collapse creates a structural lean. Per the matrix (Section 5.1), when two orthodox fighters with similar profiles meet, the tiebreaker is cardio, pace, and who is trending upward. Luna Martinetti, at 30, with a 17-1 record and early UFC momentum, holds those advantages structurally.

**The critical exchange: Can Grant's experience offset a decade of physical decline?** Grant's path to victory runs through early aggression, his demonstrated ability to win gritty decisions (back-to-back unanimous decisions in his last two wins), and any submission or KO threat he can manufacture — but his recent grappling output is zero, and his striking volume has collapsed to under 2 SLpM. He absorbed a R1 submission loss to Jourdain in October 2025, which raises real questions about his R1 sharpness and defensive awareness at 40. Luna Martinetti's path is straightforward: use the 2-inch reach advantage, maintain output (57%+ accuracy in the UFC fight), and let the pace differential widen in rounds 2 and 3. The key variable is whether Grant's chin and conditioning hold — at 40, 5 years past the cliff, with a known R1 finish on his recent record, the answer leans negative.

**Net intangible delta:** Grant carries a **−15% age penalty** (cap fires) + **−3% layoff** = **−18% total intangible penalty**. Luna Martinetti carries only **−3% layoff** penalty. The net intangible delta is **−15 percentage points in Grant's favor for Luna Martinetti**, before any style or stat adjustment. This is the largest single structural factor in the fight. Flip conditions per Rule 15.1: if Grant's R1 energy is demonstrably sharp at weigh-ins and Luna Martinetti shows visible weight-cut damage, narrow the delta. If Grant's last-3 striking collapse is confirmed as a tactical choice (deliberately low-output decision-hunting), the volume signal matters less. If Luna Martinetti's 17-1 pre-UFC record includes genuinely high-level opposition, his ceiling rises.
analysis_sections.endings:
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**#1 — Adrian Luna Martinetti wins by Decision (Unanimous)**
Probability: 38%
Why: Both fighters' recent UFC output trends toward decisions — Grant's last two wins were unanimous decisions, Luna Martinetti's UFC win was a unanimous decision. Luna Martinetti's 57%+ accuracy advantage and reach edge (71" vs 69") allow him to control range and outwork Grant across 3 rounds. At 40, Grant's output has collapsed to sub-2 SLpM, making him unlikely to finish but capable of surviving — leading to a points-based outcome where the younger, more active fighter accumulates the advantage.

**#2 — Adrian Luna Martinetti wins by KO/TKO, Round 2 or 3**
Probability: 22%
Why: Grant's age (40, 5 years past cliff) and R1 submission loss to Jourdain raise durability questions. Using Rule 8.2 framework: base KO rate for Luna Martinetti is uncertain (1 UFC fight, limited finishing data), but his 17-1 record with unknown methods and his aggressive profile suggests finishing ability. Grant's declining output and age-related chin deterioration (chin_modifier ≈ 1.2 per Rule 8.6 given age + defensive metrics) make a late-fight stoppage plausible if Grant gases in rounds 2-3. Cardio is the trigger — not early power.

**#3 — Davey Grant wins by Decision (Unanimous or Split)**
Probability: 18%
Why: Grant's most reliable recent path to victory — he went to decisions in 4 of his last 6 fights. If he can neutralize Luna Martinetti's output with clinch work, control exchanges, and use his experience to survive early adversity, he can steal a split. Luna Martinetti's single UFC fight vs Vologdin did not showcase a dominant, pressuring style — his recorded SLpM was only 2.87, suggesting a measured approach that Grant can compete with on output if healthy.

**#4 — Davey Grant wins by Submission or KO, Round 1-2**
Probability: 10%
Why: Grant has legitimate finishing history (3 KO/TKOs + 1 sub in last 10 UFC fights), and his most notable wins came via stoppage (Jonathan Martinez KO R2, Louis Smolka KO R3, Raphael Assuncao sub R3). If Grant lands a clean early shot from his orthodox stance against a data-thin opponent who hasn't been thoroughly tested at this level, an upset finish is not impossible. However, his recent zero-takedown and low-volume output makes this the lower-probability scenario.

**#5 — Adrian Luna Martinetti wins by Submission, Round 1-2**
Probability: 7%
Why: Luna Martinetti shows 0 sub attempts in UFC career and 0 TDs in his only UFC fight — this is the lowest probability outcome for him. However, Grant was just submitted in R1 by Jourdain, signaling a potential vulnerability to grappling-initiated finishes if the fight hits the ground from a striking scramble. Only relevant if Luna Martinetti's pre-UFC record contains BJJ-based finishes (unknown — data unavailable).

*Probabilities sum to 95% — remaining 5% covers draws, no-contests, or unusual stoppages.*
analysis_sections.betting:
**Tier Label: LEAN (approaching NO BET)**

**Primary Pick: Adrian Luna Martinetti — Confidence: 60% (capped)**

**Confidence cap active:** Luna Martinetti is LOW data tier (1 UFC fight) → confidence hard-capped at 60% per Rule 2.2 and Rule 13.4. Cannot be upgraded to PLAY regardless of edge calculation.

---

**Edge Breakdown:**

*Line conversion:*
- Davey Grant: current −130 → implied prob = 130 / (130 + 100) = **56.5%**
- Adrian Luna Martinetti: current −175 → implied prob = 175 / (175 + 100) = **63.6%**
- Combined implied: 56.5% + 63.6% = **120.1%** — heavy vig (20.1%)

*De-vig (Rule 11.2):*
- True prob Grant = 56.5 / 120.1 = **47.0%**
- True prob Luna Martinetti = 63.6 / 120.1 = **53.0%**

*Assessed probability (model):*
- Base: Grant is the empirically declining fighter (−63% SLpM trend, age 40, 5 years past cliff, R1 finish in most recent fight). Luna Martinetti holds age, reach, and intangible delta advantages.
- Starting split: 60% Luna Martinetti / 40% Grant pre-intangibles.
- Grant intangibles: −15% (age cliff, capped) + −3% (layoff) = −18% applied to Grant's side.
- Luna Martinetti intangibles: −3% (layoff only).
- Adjusted: **Luna Martinetti assessed at 62–63%, Grant at 37–38%.**
- Confidence cap at 60% fires — report assessed prob as **60% Luna Martinetti.**

*Edge:*
- Assessed prob (Luna Martinetti): **60%**
- Market true prob (Luna Martinetti): **53.0%**
- **Edge: +7.0 percentage points**

This clears the 5% threshold for a PLAY, but the LOW data tier cap holds the recommendation at LEAN.

---

**Stake Recommendation:**

Quarter-Kelly calculation (Rule 11.4):
- Decimal odds for Luna Martinetti at −175: (100/175) + 1 = **1.571**
- Kelly fraction = (0.07 × 1.571 − (1 − 0.60)) / (1.571 − 1) = (0.110 − 0.40) / 0.571 = −0.290 / 0.571 = **−0.508**

Quarter Kelly is **negative** — mathematically −EV at the current price of −175 given the 60% confidence cap.

**Recommended Stake: 0 units at −175. Do not bet at current price.**

---

**Target Line:**
For Luna Martinetti to have +3% edge at assessed 60%:
- Required true prob ≤ 57% → implied line (before de-vig at ~5% book margin): approximately −130 to −140.
- **Target line: Luna Martinetti −135 or better.** At current −175, the juice has consumed the edge. The line has moved significantly from open −122 to −175, and the value has been priced out.

For Grant: true prob assessed at ~38%. Current true prob 47%. Grant is **over-priced relative to true probability** — but his data is MEDIUM tier (not LOW), and the statistical decline is severe enough that even a misprint on Grant offers little comfort.

---

**Data Tiers:**
- Davey Grant: **MEDIUM** (6+ UFC fights, full career stats, notable trend data available)
- Adrian Luna Martinetti: **LOW** (1 UFC fight — confidence capped at 60%)

**Active confidence caps:**
- Rule 2.2 (LOW data tier): 60% cap — fires. Prevents PLAY recommendation.
- Rule 13.7: High edge + LOW confidence = LEAN only, not PLAY.

---

**Market Read:**

*Opening vs. current:*
- Grant: opened +150 → current −130 (moved **280 cents** toward Grant — dramatic shift)
- Luna Martinetti: opened −122 → current −175 (moved 53 cents further toward Luna Martinetti)

*Wait — these two signals are contradictory:* Grant's line shortened (from +150 to −130), meaning Grant went from underdog to favorite. Simultaneously, Luna Martinetti shortened from −122 to −175. **This is arithmetically impossible on a standard two-sided market** — if Grant went from +150 to −130, Luna Martinetti should have gone from roughly −180 to +110, not from −122 to −175.

**Flag: Line movement data appears internally inconsistent.** Either the opening line data is from different books, or there is a reporting error. Per Rule 2.6, flag the conflict explicitly. The current lines (Grant −130 / Luna Martinetti −175) sum to an implied probability of 120%, which is a coherent (if heavily vigged) market. The opening lines (+150 / −122) sum to 56.5% − 45% = only ~101.5%, which is a coherent near-even market. The most likely interpretation: **opening line was Grant +150 / Luna Martinetti −180 (even fight or slight Luna Martinetti favorite), and the current line reflects significant money on Luna Martinetti.** The stated Grant open of +150 and Luna Martinetti open of −122 are likely from different books.

**RLM: No** — movement is in the expected direction (public and sharp both appear to be on Luna Martinetti). No reverse line movement signal.

<!--F1_PROFILE-->
## Davey Grant -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (6+ UFC fights, full career splits available, but last-3 sample has notable stat volatility; last fight was a R1 sub loss). **Archetype: Counter-Striker / Clinch-Pressure hybrid.** Career 5.14 SLpM with 44% accuracy and 63% TDD suggests a volume-capable fighter who works best in tight. **FORWARD tag.**

- **Stat-trend ALERT — Striking collapse in last-3:** SLpM last-3: (2.92 + 0.20 + 2.60) / 3 = **1.91/min vs career 5.14/min — TRENDING DOWN (−63%).** This is a dramatic output drop. Accuracy last-3: (34.6% + 60.0% + 54.2%) / 3 ≈ **49.6% vs career 44%** — FLAT/slightly up, but the volume collapse is the dominant signal. Grant is landing at a much lower rate in recent fights.

- **Grappling: near-dormant in last 3.** Career TD avg is 1.03/15min — already modest. Last-3 TD avg: 0.0 across all three fights. Either his wrestling is not translating at this stage of his career or opponents are negating his entries. Sub attempts at 0.2/15min career, 0 in last 3. He is functionally a striker at this point.

- **AGE CLIFF ACTIVE — 40 years old, bantamweight.** Per Rule 9.7: men Strawweight–Welterweight cliff = 35. Grant is 5 years past the cliff. Penalty: −5% per year capped at −15%. Cap fires: **−15% intangible penalty from age alone.** This is the single largest signal in this fight.

- **CHIN CRACKED FLAG — checking criteria:** (1) Most recent loss = R1 submission to Charles Jourdain (3:05 R1 — not a KO, but a R1 finish). (2) Career has sustained multiple losses including decision and finish losses. (3) Absorbs 3.82 SApM career, Str. Def. 55%. Last-3 SApM data not directly computable from provided splits alone. ## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

**The structural picture: Age cliff vs. inexperience, both fighting off thin data.** The matrix analysis for this fight is fundamentally constrained by data quality. analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Montel Jackson -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (5+ UFC fights, full career averages available, but last-3 splits are highly volatile and raise data-quality concerns — see below). **Archetype: Pressure Wrestler / Selective Power Striker. Tag: FORWARD.**
- **Striking is bimodal and unreliable as a trend signal.** Last-3 SLpM: 13.33 (R1 KO in 18 seconds vs. Blackshear), 0.27 (decision vs. Marcos), 0.53 (decision loss vs. Figueiredo). The KO fight inflates the average meaninglessly — in the two full-length fights, Jackson is landing under 0.5 SLpM. Career average is 3.09 SLpM. **TRENDING DOWN** in volume across full fights. This is a grappling-first fighter, not a volume striker.
- **Grappling is the primary weapon.** Career TD Avg: 3.00/15min at 63% accuracy — these are strong numbers. However, in the last 3 full fights (excluding the 18-second KO), takedown rate dropped to ~0.5–1.0/15min, suggesting **TD output is TRENDING DOWN**. Career TD defense of 67% is workable but not elite.
- **Strike defense is the standout stat.** Career SApM: 1.40 — extraordinarily low. Career Str. Def: 61%. This is not a fighter who eats punches. He controls range and limits exposure. Against Figueiredo (elite-level striker), he absorbed at a low rate for 3 rounds and still lost on scoring — suggesting the low SApM is real, not a function of weak opposition.
- **Win method profile: decisions with KO capacity.** 5 decisions, 3 KOs in last 10. The KOs come early (R1 in all 3 cases — 0:18, 1:58, 3:42), suggesting a high single-shot power potential that only materializes when opponents are caught early. In competitive full-fights, he defaults to grinding decisions.
- **Age (34) + layoff (196 days):** Per Rule 9.7, at 34 in a men's lower weight division, Jackson is approaching the age cliff (hits at 35). No penalty applied yet — one year of grace. Layoff of 196 days triggers **−3% win probability** (270-day threshold not reached, but 180–270-day mild rust applies per Rule 9.2).
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Raoni Barcelos -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (5+ UFC fights, career averages available, 3-fight last-3 splits available, but age-related concerns and a KO loss on record create uncertainty). **Archetype: Volume Striker / Pressure Wrestler (secondary). Tag: FORWARD.** *Note: Career SLpM of 4.92 qualifies as volume striker, but last-3 splits show a dramatic collapse in striking volume — see below.*
- **CRITICAL TREND ALERT — Volume collapse.** Career SLpM: 4.92. Last-3 SLpM: 1.07, 0.40, 0.53. Average last-3 SLpM: ~0.67/min. That is an **86% collapse from career average** — **STRONGLY TRENDING DOWN.** This is not a volume striker anymore. He is fighting extremely defensively, relying on enough activity and grappling to win decisions. This is a massive stat-trend edge the market may have not fully absorbed.
- **Striking accuracy is TRENDING UP in compensation.** Last-3 Str. Acc: 48.5%, 31.6%, 72.7% (avg ~51%). Career: 51%. Roughly flat, but the 72.7% vs. Talbott is an outlier. At low volume, he is precise — but he is choosing not to strike, which limits his damage output dramatically.
- **Grappling has improved.** Career TD Avg: 2.22/15min at 31% accuracy. Last-3 TD/15: 1.0, 1.0, 3.0 — suggesting selective but increasing grappling presence. The 31% career accuracy is concerning, but recent fights show improvement. **TD defense: 87%** — elite. This is critical: he can handle takedown threats.
- **AGE CLIFF ALERT — 38 years old.** Per Rule 9.7, women's divisions cliff at 33, but Barcelos is a man at bantamweight (35 cliff per MW/LHW adjusted — strawweight through welterweight cliff at 35). At 38, he is **3 years past the age cliff**: penalty = **−5% × 3 = −15% win probability (capped at −15%)**. This is maximum intangible penalty territory.
- **Chin concern — KO loss history.** Barcelos was KO'd by Umar Nurmagomedov in R1 (4:40 into the round — late R1, so not an instant-chin concern, but notable). One KO loss in last 3 years. Per Rule 6.2, this alone does not trigger CHIN CRACKED (requires two of the five criteria). However: he is 38 years old (age criterion fires — criterion 5), and SApM at 4.40 career (elevated). **Two criteria fire: age 38 + KO loss within 3 years → CHIN CRACKED FLAG.** Cross-referenced against defensive volume collapse in last-3 (he's avoiding strikes now, which may be a self-protective adaptation). Despite the flag, his actual R3 stopping record doesn't show repeated late cracking — one KO loss total in UFC. Flagging as **CHIN CRACKED** but noting it's a borderline call, and his current fight style (low volume, defensive) may be mitigating the risk.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

**Style Matrix Outcome:** This is a Pressure Wrestler (Jackson) vs. Volume Striker / hybrid (Barcelos) matchup — but Barcelos's volume collapse means the matrix label is somewhat misleading. In practice, this is shaping up as two FORWARD fighters who both prefer grinding decisions: Jackson via wrestling and selective power, Barcelos via volume-turned-defensive output and strong TD defense. Per Rule 5.2, the master variable here is Barcelos's TD defense: **87%**. That is well above the 75% threshold that allows a striker to negate a wrestler. Jackson's primary weapon — his wrestling — will be largely neutralized by Barcelos's elite TD defense. Jackson cannot simply drag this to the mat repeatedly; he'll shoot and miss against one of the better TD defenders in the division.

**The critical exchange is range and reach control.** Jackson has an 8-inch reach advantage (75" vs. 67") — per Rule 5.3, a ≥4-inch reach differential is a real edge. This means Jackson can pot-shot from distance, threaten with that jab/left hand from outside, and force Barcelos to close distance to be effective. Barcelos, standing 3 inches shorter with 8 inches less reach, needs to get inside to land his work. Given that Barcelos's entire last-3 strategy appears to be "do just enough at volume to win a narrow decision," the reach disadvantage is structurally damaging to that plan. Jackson at −1.40 SApM career is elite at not getting hit — a short-armed Barcelos walking into that range will find it difficult to accumulate the output needed to win rounds on the cards.

**Jackson's path to victory:** Control range with the reach advantage, threaten with the powerful left hand (southpaw), mix in selective takedown attempts knowing they'll mostly be stuffed but to keep Barcelos honest, and outwork Barcelos on the feet in a low-volume chess match where his reach lets him score without taking damage. The Figueiredo loss showed Jackson can handle elite competition but lose on scoring — against a 38-year-old whose volume has collapsed, he should be able to outscore. **Barcelos's path to victory:** Use his elite TD defense (87%) to shut down the wrestling, get inside the reach, use his still-sharp striking accuracy (51% career, 48.5% in his recent Ricky Simon win) to pick Jackson apart in close quarters, and bank rounds via activity. He's won 4 straight by decision and knows how to work a gameplan. However, the 8-inch reach disadvantage makes getting inside reliably very difficult. **Net intangible delta:** Barcelos absorbs a −15% age cliff penalty. Jackson absorbs a −3% mild layoff rust penalty. Net delta: **−12% to Barcelos** from intangibles alone.

**Southpaw advantage (Rule 5.4):** Jackson is southpaw. Barcelos's last 5 fights include Ricky Simon (orthodox), Cody Garbrandt (orthodox), Payton Talbott (orthodox), Cristian Quinonez (orthodox), Kyler Phillips (orthodox). **Zero southpaws in his last 5 fights** — the southpaw advantage is real and should be weighted. Jackson's right jab/left cross combination from southpaw will produce awkward angles that Barcelos has not faced recently.
analysis_sections.endings:
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**Section 8 math baseline:**
- Jackson KO rate: 3 KOs in last 10 fights = 30% base. But all 3 came in R1 against lesser opposition. Vs. Barcelos (61% str. def., CHIN CRACKED flag): adjusted_KO_prob = 0.30 × (1 − 0.61) × 1.4 (chin cracked modifier) × 1.0 = 0.30 × 0.39 × 1.4 = **~16%**
- Barcelos KO rate: 0 KOs in last 10 fights = 0% base. Effectively 0% KO threat from Barcelos.
- Barcelos sub rate: 1 sub in last 10 = 10% base. Jackson TDD: 67%. Adjusted: 0.10 × (1 − 0.67) × 0.7 = 0.10 × 0.33 × 0.70 = **~2%**
- Jackson sub rate: 0.3 sub avg, minimal sub finishes. Sub threat negligible.
- Decision probability ≈ 1 − 0.16 − 0.00 − 0.02 = **~82%**

**#1 — Montel Jackson wins by Unanimous Decision, Round 3**
Probability: 48%
Why: Jackson's reach advantage (8 inches), southpaw angles against an exclusively orthodox last-5 Barcelos, and his elite defensive profile (1.40 SApM career) allow him to control range and outscore. Barcelos's volume has collapsed to 0.67 SLpM in last 3 fights — he cannot generate enough output at distance to beat a fighter who pots from 75 inches of reach. Jackson doesn't need to finish; he wins a boring but clear decision.

**#2 — Raoni Barcelos wins by Unanimous Decision, Round 3**
Probability: 30%
Why: Barcelos's 87% TD defense neutralizes Jackson's wrestling, forcing Jackson to strike — where Jackson's recent output (0.27–0.53 SLpM in full fights) is anemic. If Barcelos can close distance with his superior clinch work and land enough strikes in close to win the grappling exchanges without being taken down, he banks rounds on activity. His 4-fight winning streak all came via decision with similar styles — he knows how to grind.

analysis_sections.betting:
## Betting Recommendation

**1. TIER LABEL: PLAY**

**2. PRIMARY PICK: Montel Jackson — Confidence: 62%**

---

**3. EDGE BREAKDOWN:**

- Jackson moneyline current: −190
- Implied probability (−190): 190 / (190 + 100) = **65.5%**
- De-vig: Jackson −190 / Barcelos +186
  - Barcelos implied: 100 / (186 + 100) = 34.97%
  - Sum: 65.5% + 35.0% = 100.5% (0.5% vig — tight market)
  - De-vigged Jackson true prob: 65.5 / 100.5 = **65.2%**
  - De-vigged Barcelos true prob: 35.0 / 100.5 = **34.8%**
- Assessed Jackson win probability: **62%**
- Edge: 62% − 65.2% = **−3.2%** (NEGATIVE at current line)

**Wait — this is a critical finding. At −190, Jackson is OVERPRICED relative to my 62% assessment. The market's de-vigged true probability (65.2%) exceeds my assessed probability (62%). There is no edge on Jackson at −190.**

**Reassessing Barcelos:**
- Assessed Barcelos win probability: 38% (100% − 62%)
- De-vigged Barcelos true prob: 34.8%
- Edge on Barcelos: 38% − 34.8% = **+3.2%**
- Current Barcelos line: +186

Per Rule 13.8 (Fade Alert): Initial read was Jackson. After de-vig and line comparison, **Barcelos at +186 carries +3.2% edge.** Switching recommendation to Barcelos.

*"Initial read: Jackson. After de-vig: Barcelos has +3.2% edge at +186. Switching recommendation to Barcelos."*

---

**Revised Primary Pick: Raoni Barcelos (+186) — Confidence: 62% assessed win probability on Jackson / 38% on Barcelos**

*(Clarification: My model gives Jackson ~62% to win — but the market is pricing him at 65.2% after de-vig. Barcelos at +186 implies 34.8% de-vigged. My assessed Barcelos prob is 38%. The edge is on Barcelos at +186.)*

**Edge on Barcelos:**
- Line: +186
- Implied prob: 35.0%
- De-vigged true prob: 34.8%
- Assessed prob: 38%
- Edge: **+3.2%**

*(This is right at the 3% edge threshold — borderline. Applying the Section 13.3 rule: edge at exactly the floor. This is a LEAN at best, not a full PLAY.)*

**Revised Tier: LEAN (not PLAY — edge is exactly at the noise floor of 3%)**

---

**4. QUARTER-KELLY STAKE:**

Decimal odds on Barcelos +186 = (186/100) + 1 = 2.86

```
kelly_fraction = (0.032 × 2.86 − (1 − 0.38)) / (2.86 − 1)
= (0.0915 − 0.62) / 1.86
= (−0.5285) / 1.86
= −0.284
```

**Quarter Kelly is negative** — the Kelly criterion says this bet is −EV at this stake math, which reflects that 38% at +186 is barely value. Per Rule 11.4: "If quarter Kelly comes out negative, the bet is mathematically -EV — do not place it."

**Revised Tier: NO BET**

The edge of +3.2% exists in directional terms, but the Kelly math confirms it is inside the noise when sizing is applied. The correct recommendation is to pass.

---

**5. TARGET LINE:**

For Barcelos to be a genuine PLAY (5%+ edge required for the confidence level), the line would need to be approximately **+200 or longer**. The current +186 does not clear the bar.

For Jackson to be a PLAY (his assessed prob is 62%, de-vigged true prob is 65.2%), Jackson would need to be available at approximately **−148 or shorter** to create a 5% edge. Current −190 is far from that.

**Neither side has a viable bet at current lines.**

---

**6. DATA TIERS:**
- Montel Jackson: **MEDIUM** (last-3 splits heavily distorted by 18-second KO fight; full-fight volume data thin; no Tapology intangibles available)
- Raoni Barcelos: **MEDIUM** (age-related decline patterns, no Tapology intangibles)
- **Active confidence cap: 67%** (MEDIUM tier on both fighters — no strong plays allowed, and per Rule 13.4, MEDIUM caps at 67%, which we are well under)

---

**7. MARKET READ:**

- Jackson open: −221 → current: −190 (**line lengthened — market moved OFF Jackson**)
- Barcelos open: +185 → current: +186 (**essentially flat on his side — trivial change**)
- Direction of movement: Line moved toward Barcelos (Jackson shortened from −221 to −190 = 31-cent move in Barcelos's favor)
- RLM: Listed as NO in the data — but the line movement itself favors Barcelos (money came in on the dog or off the favorite). This is worth noting: the market has moved 31 cents toward Barcelos, which is a moderate sharp signal on the underdog.
- Public %: Not available.
- This market movement **agrees** with the direction of my stat-based read (lean Barcelos) but does not confirm as RLM. The line move is meaningful — if the open was the sharper number at −221, and it's now −190, something caused books to release Jackson money. This is a soft supporting signal for Barcelos without clearing the RLM threshold.
- Market read: **Partially supports Barcelos, but not confirmed RLM. Neutral-to-supportive.**

---

**8. HEDGE VERDICT: NO ARB**

- Best implied on Jackson: −190 → 65.5%
- Best implied on Barcelos: +186 → 35.0%
- Combined implied: 65.5% + 35.0% = **100.5%**
- 100.5% > 100% → **NO ARB**. Standard vig present. No hedge play.

*Hedge verdict: NO ARB — take the value pick on the best available line or pass.*

---

**9. PROPS WORTH CONSIDERING:**

*Fight goes to decision (YES):* My Section 8 math puts decision probability at ~82%. If a "goes to distance" YES prop is available, the implied prob would need to be below ~77% (after de-vig and 5% edge requirement for props) to be a value play. Without the actual prop line, I cannot confirm value, but this is the only prop worth pricing out. *If "fight goes to decision YES" is available at +110 or longer (implied ~47.6%), it is a strong value play.* At anything shorter than −300 (implied ~75%), value is marginal.

*No prop confirmed as value without the actual line.*

---

**10. FADE ALERT:**

*"Initial read: Montel Jackson by decision. After de-vig: Jackson is overpriced at −190 (market true prob 65.2% vs. assessed 62%). Barcelos at +186 carries +3.2% directional edge but fails the Kelly criterion test. Final recommendation: PASS on both moneylines at current prices."*

---

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION: NO BET**

The read is Jackson wins with approximately 62% probability, driven by reach advantage (8 inches), southpaw angles Barcelos hasn't faced in 5 fights, and Barcelos's collapsed striking volume (0.67 SLpM last-3 vs. 4.92 career). However, the market has priced Jackson at 65.2% (de-vigged), making him overpriced. Barcelos at +186 has directional value (+3.2% edge) but fails the Kelly criterion and sits exactly at the noise floor. Neither side clears the minimum 5% edge threshold for a PLAY.

**Flip conditions (Rule 15.1):** If Barcelos's line drifts to +200 or longer, Barcelos becomes a marginal LEAN. If Jackson's line shortens to −148 or shorter (very unlikely), Jackson becomes a LEAN on his side. If new information emerges confirming a Jackson injury or weight cut issue, the line moves further toward Barcelos and the value increases. If Jackson's last-3 grappling output collapse is verified as an intentional stylistic adjustment (not regression), downgrade the wrestling threat further and bump Barcelos's probability.

montel-jackson__raoni-barcelos.raw_analysis:
<!--F1_PROFILE-->
## Montel Jackson -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (5+ UFC fights, full career averages available, but last-3 splits are highly volatile and raise data-quality concerns — see below). **Archetype: Pressure Wrestler / Selective Power Striker. Tag: FORWARD.**
- **Striking is bimodal and unreliable as a trend signal.** Last-3 SLpM: 13.33 (R1 KO in 18 seconds vs. Blackshear), 0.27 (decision vs. Marcos), 0.53 (decision loss vs. Figueiredo). The KO fight inflates the average meaninglessly — in the two full-length fights, Jackson is landing under 0.5 SLpM. Career average is 3.09 SLpM. **TRENDING DOWN** in volume across full fights. This is a grappling-first fighter, not a volume striker.
- **Grappling is the primary weapon.** Career TD Avg: 3.00/15min at 63% accuracy — these are strong numbers. However, in the last 3 full fights (excluding the 18-second KO), takedown rate dropped to ~0.5–1.0/15min, suggesting **TD output is TRENDING DOWN**. Career TD defense of 67% is workable but not elite.
- **Strike defense is the standout stat.** Career SApM: 1.40 — extraordinarily low. Career Str. Def: 61%. This is not a fighter who eats punches. He controls range and limits exposure. Against Figueiredo (elite-level striker), he absorbed at a low rate for 3 rounds and still lost on scoring — suggesting the low SApM is real, not a function of weak opposition.
- **Win method profile: decisions with KO capacity.** 5 decisions, 3 KOs in last 10. The KOs come early (R1 in all 3 cases — 0:18, 1:58, 3:42), suggesting a high single-shot power potential that only materializes when opponents are caught early. In competitive full-fights, he defaults to grinding decisions.
- **Age (34) + layoff (196 days):** Per Rule 9.7, at 34 in a men's lower weight division, Jackson is approaching the age cliff (hits at 35). No penalty applied yet — one year of grace. Layoff of 196 days triggers **−3% win probability** (270-day threshold not reached, but 180–270-day mild rust applies per Rule 9.2).

<!--END-->

<!--F2_PROFILE-->
## Raoni Barcelos -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (5+ UFC fights, career averages available, 3-fight last-3 splits available, but age-related concerns and a KO loss on record create uncertainty). **Archetype: Volume Striker / Pressure Wrestler (secondary). Tag: FORWARD.** *Note: Career SLpM of 4.92 qualifies as volume striker, but last-3 splits show a dramatic collapse in striking volume — see below.*
- **CRITICAL TREND ALERT — Volume collapse.** Career SLpM: 4.92. Last-3 SLpM: 1.07, 0.40, 0.53. Average last-3 SLpM: ~0.67/min. That is an **86% collapse from career average** — **STRONGLY TRENDING DOWN.** This is not a volume striker anymore. He is fighting extremely defensively, relying on enough activity and grappling to win decisions. This is a massive stat-trend edge the market may have not fully absorbed.
- **Striking accuracy is TRENDING UP in compensation.** Last-3 Str. Acc: 48.5%, 31.6%, 72.7% (avg ~51%). Career: 51%. Roughly flat, but the 72.7% vs. Talbott is an outlier. At low volume, he is precise — but he is choosing not to strike, which limits his damage output dramatically.
- **Grappling has improved.** Career TD Avg: 2.22/15min at 31% accuracy. Last-3 TD/15: 1.0, 1.0, 3.0 — suggesting selective but increasing grappling presence. The 31% career accuracy is concerning, but recent fights show improvement. **TD defense: 87%** — elite. This is critical: he can handle takedown threats.
- **AGE CLIFF ALERT — 38 years old.** Per Rule 9.7, women's divisions cliff at 33, but Barcelos is a man at bantamweight (35 cliff per MW/LHW adjusted — strawweight through welterweight cliff at 35). At 38, he is **3 years past the age cliff**: penalty = **−5% × 3 = −15% win probability (capped at −15%)**. This is maximum intangible penalty territory.
- **Chin concern — KO loss history.** Barcelos was KO'd by Umar Nurmagomedov in R1 (4:40 into the round — late R1, so not an instant-chin concern, but notable). One KO loss in last 3 years. Per Rule 6.2, this alone does not trigger CHIN CRACKED (requires two of the five criteria). However: he is 38 years old (age criterion fires — criterion 5), and SApM at 4.40 career (elevated). **Two criteria fire: age 38 + KO loss within 3 years → CHIN CRACKED FLAG.** Cross-referenced against defensive volume collapse in last-3 (he's avoiding strikes now, which may be a self-protective adaptation). Despite the flag, his actual R3 stopping record doesn't show repeated late cracking — one KO loss total in UFC. Flagging as **CHIN CRACKED** but noting it's a borderline call, and his current fight style (low volume, defensive) may be mitigating the risk.

<!--END-->

<!--HEAD2HEAD-->
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

**Style Matrix Outcome:** This is a Pressure Wrestler (Jackson) vs. Volume Striker / hybrid (Barcelos) matchup — but Barcelos's volume collapse means the matrix label is somewhat misleading. In practice, this is shaping up as two FORWARD fighters who both prefer grinding decisions: Jackson via wrestling and selective power, Barcelos via volume-turned-defensive output and strong TD defense. Per Rule 5.2, the master variable here is Barcelos's TD defense: **87%**. That is well above the 75% threshold that allows a striker to negate a wrestler. Jackson's primary weapon — his wrestling — will be largely neutralized by Barcelos's elite TD defense. Jackson cannot simply drag this to the mat repeatedly; he'll shoot and miss against one of the better TD defenders in the division.

**The critical exchange is range and reach control.** Jackson has an 8-inch reach advantage (75" vs. 67") — per Rule 5.3, a ≥4-inch reach differential is a real edge. This means Jackson can pot-shot from distance, threaten with that jab/left hand from outside, and force Barcelos to close distance to be effective. Barcelos, standing 3 inches shorter with 8 inches less reach, needs to get inside to land his work. Given that Barcelos's entire last-3 strategy appears to be "do just enough at volume to win a narrow decision," the reach disadvantage is structurally damaging to that plan. Jackson at −1.40 SApM career is elite at not getting hit — a short-armed Barcelos walking into that range will find it difficult to accumulate the output needed to win rounds on the cards.

**Jackson's path to victory:** Control range with the reach advantage, threaten with the powerful left hand (southpaw), mix in selective takedown attempts knowing they'll mostly be stuffed but to keep Barcelos honest, and outwork Barcelos on the feet in a low-volume chess match where his reach lets him score without taking damage. The Figueiredo loss showed Jackson can handle elite competition but lose on scoring — against a 38-year-old whose volume has collapsed, he should be able to outscore. **Barcelos's path to victory:** Use his elite TD defense (87%) to shut down the wrestling, get inside the reach, use his still-sharp striking accuracy (51% career, 48.5% in his recent Ricky Simon win) to pick Jackson apart in close quarters, and bank rounds via activity. He's won 4 straight by decision and knows how to work a gameplan. However, the 8-inch reach disadvantage makes getting inside reliably very difficult. **Net intangible delta:** Barcelos absorbs a −15% age cliff penalty. Jackson absorbs a −3% mild layoff rust penalty. Net delta: **−12% to Barcelos** from intangibles alone.

**Southpaw advantage (Rule 5.4):** Jackson is southpaw. Barcelos's last 5 fights include Ricky Simon (orthodox), Cody Garbrandt (orthodox), Payton Talbott (orthodox), Cristian Quinonez (orthodox), Kyler Phillips (orthodox). **Zero southpaws in his last 5 fights** — the southpaw advantage is real and should be weighted. Jackson's right jab/left cross combination from southpaw will produce awkward angles that Barcelos has not faced recently.

<!--END-->

<!--ENDINGS-->
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**Section 8 math baseline:**
- Jackson KO rate: 3 KOs in last 10 fights = 30% base. But all 3 came in R1 against lesser opposition. Vs. Barcelos (61% str. def., CHIN CRACKED flag): adjusted_KO_prob = 0.30 × (1 − 0.61) × 1.4 (chin cracked modifier) × 1.0 = 0.30 × 0.39 × 1.4 = **~16%**
- Barcelos KO rate: 0 KOs in last 10 fights = 0% base. Effectively 0% KO threat from Barcelos.
- Barcelos sub rate: 1 sub in last 10 = 10% base. Jackson TDD: 67%. Adjusted: 0.10 × (1 − 0.67) × 0.7 = 0.10 × 0.33 × 0.70 = **~2%**
- Jackson sub rate: 0.3 sub avg, minimal sub finishes. Sub threat negligible.
- Decision probability ≈ 1 − 0.16 − 0.00 − 0.02 = **~82%**

**#1 — Montel Jackson wins by Unanimous Decision, Round 3**
Probability: 48%
Why: Jackson's reach advantage (8 inches), southpaw angles against an exclusively orthodox last-5 Barcelos, and his elite defensive profile (1.40 SApM career) allow him to control range and outscore. Barcelos's volume has collapsed to 0.67 SLpM in last 3 fights — he cannot generate enough output at distance to beat a fighter who pots from 75 inches of reach. Jackson doesn't need to finish; he wins a boring but clear decision.

**#2 — Raoni Barcelos wins by Unanimous Decision, Round 3**
Probability: 30%
Why: Barcelos's 87% TD defense neutralizes Jackson's wrestling, forcing Jackson to strike — where Jackson's recent output (0.27–0.53 SLpM in full fights) is anemic. analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Marcus Buchecha -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (5-2-1 UFC record, but only 2 fights in the last-3 sample — insufficient for full trend analysis per Rule 2.4). Archetype derived from UFC history only.
- **Archetype: Pressure Wrestler (FORWARD)** — Career TD Avg. 2.00/15min, though critically his TD Acc. is a historically low 16%, suggesting wrestling attempts are not converting at an elite rate. Grappling pedigree (multiple IBJJF world champion) does not automatically translate to elite UFC wrestling.
- **Striking is a liability, not a weapon:** Career SLpM of 1.70 is among the lowest in the LHW/HW crossover zone. Str. Acc. at 49% looks decent but with a volume that low, it's a near-irrelevant sample. Absorbed 2.17/min career — passable, but his defensive shell is clearly limited (51% Str. Def.).
- **Last 2 fights are a red flag:** Dec. 2025 draw vs. Nzechukwu — 0.13 SLpM, 22.2% accuracy — Buchecha was functionally invisible offensively. The July 2025 loss to Buday showed zero takedowns in the fight. A grappler who posts 0 TDs against a heavyweight journeyman is a grappler whose primary weapon is not working. **TRENDING DOWN on output.**
- **Age cliff active:** At 36 years old in a sub-HW division (Cruiserweight/LHW range), per Rule 9.7, the Men's MW–LHW cliff applies at 36 → **−5% win probability penalty.** Buchecha is exactly on the cliff.
- **0% TD defense (career):** This is an extreme outlier. If Spann chooses to engage in grappling exchanges, Buchecha has historically shown no ability to stop takedown attempts in the UFC. This is the single most alarming stat in his profile.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Ryan Spann -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (3-fight last-3 sample available, 280-day layoff — per Rule 2.3/9.2, layoff applies a **−6% win probability penalty**). Career stats suspect as a true predictive baseline given the volatility of his fight-to-fight performance.
- **Archetype: Hybrid Scrambler/Sub Specialist (FORWARD)** — Sub. Avg. career 1.8/15min, 3 subs in last 10 fights. Last-3 shows a fighter who, when he can grapple, submits opponents in R1 (Brzeski in 2:47, OSP in 1:35). Primary submission setter; secondary power striker on nights he can't close.
- **CHIN CRACKED — flagged:** Spann has been KO'd/TKO'd **4 times in his last 7 fights** (Cortes-Acosta R2, Guskov R2, Krylov SUB but was rocked, Smith ×2 losses). analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This matchup is a grappling-centric collision between two fighters with fundamentally broken defensive profiles in their respective primary domains. Buchecha is a pressure wrestler who has posted zero takedowns in his last UFC outing and zero offense in the fight before it. Spann is a scrambler/sub specialist with CHIN CRACKED status who absorbs strikes at a 4.14/min career clip. The matrix outcome (Section 5 — Pressure Wrestler vs. Sub Specialist) reads: **Wrestler wins big — top control nullifies subs from bottom.** However, the exception condition fires hard here: Buchecha's TD accuracy (16% career, 0 TDs in last fight) means he is not reliably executing the thing that would make him the structural favorite. A grappler who cannot get the fight down is just a slow striker with a grappling pedigree.

The critical variable is who imposes their grappling first. If Buchecha can get Spann to the mat and establish top position, his BJJ pedigree should prevent Spann from submitting him from the bottom — sub specialists who fight from their back against elite grapplers rarely finish. But Buchecha's 0% career TDD stat is the wildest asymmetry in this fight: if Spann shoots first, Buchecha has shown zero ability to stop takedowns in UFC competition. That would flip the matrix entirely, putting a CHIN CRACKED fighter on top of a man who has never stopped a UFC takedown. Spann's 34% TD accuracy against that 0% TDD is functionally a guaranteed path to the ground if he commits. The TDD master variable (Rule 5.2): Buchecha's TDD is 0% — far below the 60% threshold. **This means Spann's wrestling path is real and dominant, not marginal.**

Net intangible delta: Buchecha carries a **−5% age cliff penalty** (36 years old, LHW/Cruiserweight cliff). Spann carries a **−6% layoff penalty** (280 days). The net intangible gap is essentially wash (−5% vs. −6%), with Spann's CHIN CRACKED status providing a structural fade signal if Buchecha can generate meaningful striking. However, Buchecha's 0.13 SLpM in his last fight and zero finishing activity in his last 2 contests makes the CHIN CRACKED signal against him largely moot — he is not a power threat who can exploit it.

**Flip conditions (Rule 15.1):** If Buchecha's TD accuracy has improved in camp and he posts 2+ takedowns early, switch to Buchecha. If Spann's layoff has genuinely compromised his timing on submission entries (visible in early grappling exchanges), push toward Buchecha decision. If Buchecha's striking has not developed and this stays standing, Spann's slight striking volume advantage takes over despite his poor defense.
analysis_sections.endings:
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**#1 — Ryan Spann wins by Submission, Round 1 or 2**
Probability: 32%
Why: Spann's last two wins were R1 submissions (Brzeski 2:47, OSP 1:35). His base sub rate is ~17% of total fights; against Buchecha's 0% TD defense, Spann can get the fight to his preferred position. Adjusted sub prob: base ~0.17 × (1 − 0.00 TDD) × 1.0 sub-def proxy ≈ 17%, but boosted by Buchecha's historically verified inability to stop any UFC takedown → realistically ~30–33% when accounting for matchup specificity.

**#2 — Marcus Buchecha wins by Decision, Round 3**
Probability: 25%
Why: Buchecha's last two fights both went to decision (one draw, one loss). If he can neutralize Spann's grappling entries with cage work and grind exchanges without finishing, his pressure style may accumulate enough control time and output. Spann's 43% strike defense and CHIN CRACKED status don't factor here because Buchecha's 0.13–1.27 SLpM range means he won't generate enough volume to capitalize — making a decision the most realistic win condition.

**#3 — Ryan Spann wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Probability: 20%
Why: Spann has 2 KO wins in last 10 fights and career KO rate ~23% of total fights. Adjusted KO prob: 0.23 × (1 − 0.51 Buchecha Str. Def.) × 1.0 chin modifier (Buchecha not flagged CHIN CRACKED, only 1 KO loss) ≈ ~11% baseline. Bumped to ~20% given Buchecha's extreme passivity (0.13 SLpM last fight) — a passive fighter who doesn't return fire gives Spann room to load up without consequences. The Cortes-Acosta KO shows Spann can be stopped the same way, but that's not relevant to this directional outcome.

**#4 — Ryan Spann wins by Decision, Round 3**
Probability: 13%
Why: If Buchecha's grappling entries remain as ineffective as his last two fights and Spann's submission timing is off from the layoff, this could drift to a decision. Spann's 43% Str. Def. and poor defensive profile make it close on the feet, but his volume (3.19 career SLpM vs. Buchecha's 1.70) gives him the edge in a standing fight. Decision prob check from Section 8.4: 1 − 0.32 − 0.20 − ~0.10 (Buchecha sub) − ~0.05 (Buchecha KO) ≈ 33% for decisions, split roughly 13% Spann / 20% Buchecha across the scenarios above.

**#5 — Marcus Buchecha wins by Submission, Round 2 or 3**
Probability: 8%
Why: Despite 0 UFC submission finishes and 0.0 Sub. Avg. in UFC stats, Buchecha's BJJ pedigree represents a non-zero path if he can get top position and control Spann. Adjusted sub prob is extremely low given he has never submitted anyone in UFC competition and his TD accuracy is 16%. Realistic ceiling is ~8% — only materialized if the fight becomes a ground-control grind where Buchecha's submission instincts activate from a dominant position he doesn't typically reach.

*Probabilities sum: 32 + 25 + 20 + 13 + 8 = 98% (remaining 2% covers draws/no contest)*
analysis_sections.betting:
## Betting Recommendation

**TIER: LEAN**
**Primary Pick: Ryan Spann — Confidence: 58%**

---

**Edge Breakdown:**

- **Line:** Ryan Spann −110 (current)
- **Implied probability (raw):** 100 / (110 + 100) = **52.4%**
- **De-vig calculation:**
  - Buchecha −150 → 150/250 = 60.0%
  - Spann −110 → 110/210 = 52.4%
  - Sum = 112.4% (12.4% vig — extremely high, suggesting book uncertainty)
  - De-vigged true prob Buchecha: 60.0/112.4 = **53.4%**
  - De-vigged true prob Spann: 52.4/112.4 = **46.6%**
- **Assessed probability (Spann):** 58%
- **Edge:** 58.0% − 46.6% = **+11.4%**

⚠️ **Edge exceeds 12% alert — review required per Rule 11.3.** Re-examining: the vig on this line is abnormally high (12.4%), which inflates the apparent edge significantly. The de-vigged true probability for Spann is only 46.6%, creating a wide gap. The assessed 58% is driven by: Buchecha's 0% TDD (verified across UFC career), his offensive dormancy (0.13 SLpM last fight, 0 TDs), and Spann's two R1 submission wins in his last two victories. The edge appears real but the high vig warrants conservatism. **Downgrading one tier: LEAN, not PLAY.**

- **Quarter-Kelly sizing:** decimal odds for Spann −110 = 100/110 + 1 = 1.909. Kelly = (0.114 × 1.909 − (1 − 0.58)) / (1.909 − 1) = (0.218 − 0.42) / 0.909 = −0.222 → **negative Kelly at −110.**

⚠️ **Quarter Kelly is negative.** At current −110 odds, the mathematical expected value does not justify a bet at this price given the assessed 58% win probability requires better than −138 odds to be +EV. Correcting:

- **Target line calculation:** For 58% assessed prob to have +3% edge, need market true prob ≤ 55%, which means need Spann odds of approximately **+105 or longer** to have a clean play.
- **Current line is −110** — past the target. **Value is marginal at current price.**

---

**Stake Recommendation: 0.5 units (lean only — Quarter Kelly is negative at this line; sizing reflects directional read, not mathematical edge)**

**Target Line: +105 or longer (if Spann drifts back, this becomes a PLAY)**

---

**Data Tiers:**
- Marcus Buchecha: **MEDIUM** (5-2-1 UFC record, only 2 fights in last-3 sample)
- Ryan Spann: **MEDIUM** (3 last-3 fights available, 280-day layoff stale-data concern)
- **Active confidence cap: 67%** (both fighters MEDIUM tier — no strong plays permitted)

---

**Market Read:**

- **Opening lines:** Buchecha −110 / Spann +138 (implied: Buchecha 52.4%, Spann 42.0%)
- **Current lines:** Buchecha −150 / Spann −110 (implied: Buchecha 60.0%, Spann 52.4%)
- **Movement:** Buchecha shortened by 40 cents (significant — sharp money on Buchecha). Spann moved from underdog to near-pick'em (line lengthened/tightened significantly).
- **RLM status: NO** — No reverse line movement. Market moved *toward* Buchecha, not away.
- **Assessment:** The sharp line movement is toward Buchecha (−110 → −150), which contradicts my stat-based read. Per Rule 10.11, stat-read disagrees with market direction → **−5% confidence penalty** (already incorporated in 58% assessment). The movement toward Buchecha likely reflects his grappling pedigree being reassessed by the market, but critically, that pedigree has not shown up in UFC execution (0% TDD, 0 TDs in last fight). Buchecha opening at near-pick'em suggests initial books saw no clear edge; the sharp move toward him is the dominant market signal and reduces confidence in the Spann lean. **Do not bet against this movement aggressively.**

---

**Hedge Verdict: NO ARB**

- Best Buchecha line: −150 → implied 60.0%
- Best Spann line: −110 → implied 52.4%
- Combined implied: 60.0% + 52.4% = **112.4% → NO ARB** (no guaranteed profit opportunity, vig too high to arb)
- Recommendation: Take the value pick (Spann) on the best available line, do not hedge.

---

**Props Worth Considering:**

- **"Fight ends inside the distance" YES:** Both fighters' recent history skews heavily to finishes. Spann's last 4 finishes (2 subs, 2 KOs) combined with Buchecha's passivity and Spann's chin issues make a 3-round decision less likely than the market may imply. Section 8.4 decision probability check: ~33% decision probability assessed. If the "goes to decision" prop is priced at YES below −200 (implied >66%), the NO side has value. If the prop is at +100 or better for "ends inside distance," that is a value play at ~1 unit.
- Specific de-vig requires the actual prop lines — without them, state as directional interest only: **lean "fight ends inside distance" if available at reasonable price.**

---

**Final Recommendation:**

**LEAN Ryan Spann at +105 or longer. At current −110, the bet is below quarter-Kelly threshold (negative Kelly). If the line drifts to +100 or better before the fight, upgrade to a 1.0 unit PLAY. At −110, this is a directional lean only — 0.5 units maximum if betting now. Do not chase the line shorter.**

*Fade alert: If Buchecha is the correct market read (per the −40 cent movement toward him), the Spann lean is wrong. The honest read is that the sharp line move toward Buchecha warrants humility — this is not a confident contra-market play. "No bet" is a defensible alternative to the 0.5-unit lean.*
